revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''神埼荘'''（かんざきのしょう）は、[[平安時代]]～[[室町時代]]にかけて[[肥前国]]にあった荘園。神崎荘とも。皇室領。...'
revision.text:
'''神埼荘'''（かんざきのしょう）は、[[平安時代]]～[[室町時代]]にかけて[[肥前国]]にあった荘園。神崎荘とも。皇室領。
== 概要 ==
=== 所在 === 肥前国[[神埼郡]]。現在の[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]]。
=== 規模 === [[正応]]5年([[1291年]])に3000町。
=== 起源 === [[承和]]3年([[836年]])に勅旨田になった690町が起源で、[[長和]]4年([[1015年]])以前に立荘、皇室領として歴代天皇・上皇に相伝された。
=== 領主 === 皇室領([[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]→[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]ら)→三浦氏([[三浦泰村]])→皇室領([[後嵯峨天皇|後嵯峨上皇]]ら)
=== 終焉 ===
[[元寇|文永・弘安の役]]で恩賞地配分の対象とされたため細分化され、[[南北朝時代]]には名前だけを残して荘園としての実態は失われた。
== 備考 == [[平安時代]]には[[日宋貿易]]の拠点となった。
== 関連項目 == *[[荘園の一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『國史大辭典』([[吉川弘文館]]) *『日本史広辞典』([[山川出版社]])
[[Category:佐賀県の歴史|かんさきのしよう]] [[Category:平安時代|かんさきのしよう]] [[Category:鎌倉時代|かんさきのしよう]] [[Category:肥前国|かんさきのしよう]] [[Category:日本の荘園制|かんさきのしよう]]

=== 領主 === 皇室領([[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]→[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]ら)→三浦氏([[三浦泰村]])→皇室領([[後嵯峨天皇|後嵯峨上皇]]ら)

== 備考 == [[平安時代]]には[[日宋貿易]]の拠点となった。
== 関連項目 == *[[荘園の一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『國史大辭典』([[吉川弘文館]]) *『日本史広辞典』([[山川出版社]])

revision.comment: レンネン がページ「[[神崎荘]]」を「[[神埼荘]]」に移動しました: 誤字のため
revision.text:
'''神埼荘'''（かんざきのしょう）は、[[平安時代]]～[[室町時代]]にかけて[[肥前国]]にあった荘園。神崎荘とも。皇室領。
== 概要 ==
=== 所在 === 肥前国[[神埼郡]]。現在の[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]]。
=== 規模 === [[正応]]5年([[1291年]])に3000町。
=== 起源 === [[承和 (日本)|承和]]3年([[836年]])に勅旨田になった690町が起源で、[[長和]]4年([[1015年]])以前に立荘、皇室領として歴代天皇・上皇に相伝された。
=== 領主 === 皇室領([[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]→[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]ら)→三浦氏([[三浦泰村]])→皇室領([[後嵯峨天皇|後嵯峨上皇]]ら)
=== 終焉 ===
[[元寇|文永・弘安の役]]で恩賞地配分の対象とされたため細分化され、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には名前だけを残して荘園としての実態は失われた。
== 備考 == [[平安時代]]には[[日宋貿易]]の拠点となった。
== 関連項目 == * [[日宋貿易]] * [[院政]] * [[平氏政権]] * [[源満末]] * [[日本の荘園の一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『国史大辞典』([[吉川弘文館]]) *『日本史広辞典』([[山川出版社]])
{{DEFAULTSORT:かんさきのしよう}} [[Category:佐賀県の歴史]] [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:鎌倉時代]] [[Category:肥前国]] [[Category:日本の荘園]]

'''神埼荘'''（かんざきのしょう）は、[[平安時代]]から[[室町時代]]にかけて[[肥前国]]にあった荘園。神崎荘とも。代々皇室領。
== 概要 ==
=== 所在 === 肥前国[[神埼郡]]にあったとされる（現在の[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]]付近）
=== 規模 === [[正応]]5年（[[1291年]]）時点、3000町。
=== 起源 === [[承和 (日本)|承和]]3年（[[836年]]）に勅旨田になった690町が起源である。
[[長和]]4年([[1015年]])以前に立荘、皇室領として歴代天皇・上皇に相伝された。
=== 領主 === 皇室領([[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]→[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]ら)→三浦氏([[三浦泰村]])→皇室領([[後嵯峨天皇|後嵯峨上皇]]ら)
=== 歴史と衰微 === 奈良・飛鳥時代以前の古代は、神崎荘の後背山地、[[脊振山地|脊振山]]はヤマトや朝廷の支配が及ばない秘境であり縄文系山人（[[オオヤマツミ]]など縄文神由来の山岳信仰集団）が住んでいた。
9世紀頃の神埼荘開発後、背振山門の[[鹿路氏]]や[[廣滝氏]]が経営に関わる。中央から下向してきた藤原氏や平氏の一門もこの地に土着した。藤原氏流[[三瀬氏]]など。[[平安時代]]には[[日宋貿易]]の拠点となり、[[平忠盛]]一族の知行となる
[[刀伊の入寇]]後の11 - 12世紀頃、[[鎮西平氏]]の一門[[伊佐平次兼輔]]（[[平兼輔]]）が神埼荘に土着、神埼氏（神埼兼輔）を称したと伝わる。平兼輔は島津荘を開墾したとされる[[大宰府|大宰大監]][[平季基]]の子と伝わる。また、[[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]3年（[[1168年]]）に[[荘官]]として[[嵯峨源氏]][[源融|融流]]の[[源満末]]が入る。満末の子孫は[[筑後国]][[三潴郡]][[蒲池]]の[[地頭]]となり[[蒲池氏]]を称する。この頃脊振千坊も栄える（[[霊仙寺跡]]など）。
[[元寇|文永・弘安の役]]で神埼荘は恩賞地配分の対象とされたため細分化された。三島氏族（[[大山祇神社]]）の[[河野氏]]などが入る。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には名前だけを残して荘園としての実態は失われた。
戦国時代に入り肥前に[[龍造寺氏]]が台頭、[[川上峡合戦]]で破れ降り背振山権門は歴史の表舞台からは姿を消した（「[[神代勝利]]」参照）。
== 関連項目 == * [[日宋貿易]] * [[院政]] * [[平氏政権]] * [[源満末]] * [[日本の荘園の一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『国史大辞典』([[吉川弘文館]]) *『日本史広辞典』([[山川出版社]])
{{DEFAULTSORT:かんさきのしよう}} [[Category:佐賀県の歴史]] [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:鎌倉時代]] [[Category:肥前国]] [[Category:日本の荘園]]

== 参考文献 == *『国史大辞典』([[吉川弘文館]]) *『日本史広辞典』([[山川出版社]])
{{DEFAULTSORT:かんさきのしよう}} [[Category:佐賀県の歴史]] [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:鎌倉時代]] [[Category:肥前国]] [[Category:日本の荘園]]

'''神埼荘'''（かんざきのしょう）は、[[平安時代]]から[[室町時代]]にかけて[[肥前国]]にあった荘園。神崎荘とも。代々皇室領。
== 概要 ==
=== 所在 === 肥前国[[神埼郡]]にあったとされる（現在の[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]]付近）
=== 規模 === [[正応]]5年（[[1291年]]）時点、3000町。
=== 起源 === [[承和 (日本)|承和]]3年（[[836年]]）に[[勅旨田]]になった690町が起源である。
[[長和]]4年（[[1015年]]）以前に立荘、皇室領として歴代天皇・上皇に相伝された。
=== 領主 === 皇室領([[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]→[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]ら)→三浦氏([[三浦泰村]])→皇室領([[後嵯峨天皇|後嵯峨上皇]]ら)
=== 歴史と衰微 ===
9世紀の勅旨田荘開発後、背振山門の[[鹿路氏]]や[[廣滝氏]]が経営に関わる。中央から下向してきた藤原氏や平氏の一門もこの地に土着した。藤原氏流[[三瀬氏]]など。[[平安時代]]には[[日宋貿易]]の拠点となり、[[平忠盛]]一族の知行となる
[[刀伊の入寇]]後の11 - 12世紀頃、[[鎮西平氏]]の一門[[伊佐平次兼輔]]（[[平兼輔]]）が神埼荘に土着、[[神埼氏]]（神埼兼輔）を称したと伝わる（平兼輔は島津荘を開墾したとされる[[大宰府|大宰大監]][[平季基]]の子と伝わる）。また、[[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]3年（[[1168年]]）に[[荘官]]として[[嵯峨源氏]][[源融|融流]]の[[源満末]]が入る。満末の子孫は[[筑後国]][[三潴郡]][[蒲池]]の[[地頭]]となり[[蒲池氏]]を称する。この頃脊振千坊も栄える（[[霊仙寺跡]]など）。
[[元寇|文永・弘安の役]]で神埼荘は恩賞地配分の対象とされたため細分化された。三島氏族（[[大山祇神社]]）の[[河野氏]]などが入る。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には名前だけを残して荘園としての実態は失われた。
戦国時代に入り肥前に[[龍造寺氏]]が台頭、[[川上峡合戦]]で破れ降り背振山権門は歴史の表舞台からは姿を消した（「[[神代勝利]]」参照）。
== 関連項目 == * [[日宋貿易]] * [[院政]] * [[平氏政権]] * [[源満末]] * [[日本の荘園の一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『国史大辞典』([[吉川弘文館]]) *『日本史広辞典』([[山川出版社]])
{{DEFAULTSORT:かんさきのしよう}} [[Category:九州地方の荘園]] [[Category:佐賀県の歴史]] [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:鎌倉時代]] [[Category:肥前国]]

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō'''（神埼荘）は、{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}から{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}にかけて{{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}}にあった{{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}}。神崎荘とも。代々皇室領。
== 概要 ==
=== 所在 ===
肥前国{{ill|Kanzaki District|ja|神埼郡}}にあったとされる（現在の{{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}{{ill|Kanzaki City|ja|神埼市}}付近）
=== 規模 ===
{{ill|Shōō|ja|正応}}5年（{{ill|1291|ja|1291年}}）時点、3000町。
=== 起源 ===
{{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}}3年（{{ill|836|ja|836年}}）に{{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}}になった690町が起源である。
{{ill|Chōwa (Japan)|ja|長和}}4年（{{ill|1015|ja|1015年}}）以前に立荘、皇室領として歴代天皇・上皇に相伝された。
=== 領主 ===
皇室領（{{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}}→{{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}ら）→{{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}}（{{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}）→皇室領（{{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}ら）
=== 歴史と衰微 ===
9世紀の勅旨田荘開発後、{{ill|Sefuri Sanmon|ja|背振山門}}の{{ill|Karaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}}や{{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}が経営に関わる。中央から下向してきた{{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}や{{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}の一門もこの地に土着した。藤原氏流{{ill|Mitsuse clan|ja|三瀬氏}}など。{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}から{{ill|Heike administration|ja|平氏政権}}期にかけて、大宰府、薩摩阿多と併せ{{ill|Japan–Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}}の拠点となり、{{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}一族の知行となる。
{{ill|Toi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}}後の11 - 12世紀頃、{{ill|Chinzei Heishi|ja|鎮西平氏}}の一門{{ill|Isasa Heiji Kanesuke|ja|伊佐平次兼輔}}（{{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}}）が神埼荘に土着、{{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}（神埼兼輔）を称したと伝わる（平兼輔は島津荘を開墾したとされる{{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}}の{{ill|Chinjōkan|ja|大宰大監}}{{ill|Taira no Suemoto|ja|平季基}}の子と伝わる）。また、{{ill|Ninan|ja|仁安}}3年（{{ill|1168|ja|1168年}}）に{{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}}として{{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}}{{ill|Minamoto no Tōru|ja|融流}}の{{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}が入る。満末の子孫は{{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}}{{ill|Mizuma District|ja|三潴郡}}{{ill|Kamachi|ja|蒲池}}の{{ill|Jitō|ja|地頭}}となり{{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}を称する。この頃脊振千坊も栄える（{{ill|Raisen-ji temple ruins|ja|霊仙寺跡}}など）。
{{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}（{{ill|Bun'ei|ja|文永}}・{{ill|Kōan|ja|弘安}}の役）で神埼荘は恩賞地配分の対象とされたため細分化された。{{ill|Mishima clan|ja|三島氏族}}（{{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}}）の{{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}}などが入る。
{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}には名前だけを残して荘園としての実態は失われた。
戦国時代に入り肥前に{{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}}が台頭、{{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}で敗れ降り背振山権門は歴史の表舞台からは姿を消した（「{{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}」参照）。
== 参考文献 == *『{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}』（{{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}） *『{{ill|Nihonshi Hirojiten|ja|日本史広辞典}}』（{{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}）
== 関連項目 == * {{ill|Japan–Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Heike administration|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of Shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

[[ja:神埼荘]]

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}

=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.

It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.

Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}.
