{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧}}

Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利}}).

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡|12=draft}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応|12=draft}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=draft}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和|12=draft}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和|12=draft}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=draft}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏|12=draft}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=draft}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=draft}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=draft}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=draft}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府|12=draft}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛|12=draft}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇|12=draft}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=draft}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=draft}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=draft}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=draft}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=draft}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=draft}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|12=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=draft}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=draft}}

In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=draft|12=draft}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft|12=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=draft|12=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=draft|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|12=draft|12=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=draft|12=draft}}}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "{{draft categories|↵↵" to "{{draft categories|"
revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=draft|12=draft}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=draft|12=draft}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=draft|12=draft}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府|12=draft|12=draft}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=draft|12=draft}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft|12=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=draft|12=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=draft|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|12=draft|12=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=draft|12=draft}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=draft|12=draft}}

It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=draft|12=draft}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=draft|12=draft}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=draft|12=draft}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府|12=draft|12=draft}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=draft|12=draft}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft|12=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=draft|12=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=draft|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|12=draft|12=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=draft|12=draft}}}}

In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=draft|12=draft}}}}

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=draft|12=draft}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=draft|12=draft}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=draft|12=draft}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府|12=draft|12=draft}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=draft|12=draft}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft|12=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=draft|12=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=draft|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|12=draft|12=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=draft|12=draft}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} <!--Kanzaki Manor--> <!--Kanzaki Manor-->

Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府|12=draft|12=draft}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=draft|12=draft}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft|12=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=draft|12=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=draft|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|12=draft|12=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=draft|12=draft}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} <!--Kanzaki Manor--> <!--Kanzaki Manor-->

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''神埼荘'''（かんざきのしょう）は、[[平安時代]]から[[室町時代]]にかけて[[肥前国]]にあった荘園。神崎荘とも。代々皇室領。
== 概要 == === 所在 === 肥前国[[神埼郡]]にあったとされる（現在の[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]]付近）
=== 規模 === [[正応]]5年（[[1291年]]）時点、3000町。
=== 起源 === [[承和 (日本)|承和]]3年（[[836年]]）に[[勅旨田]]になった690町が起源である。
[[長和]]4年（[[1015年]]）以前に立荘、皇室領として歴代天皇・上皇に相伝された。
=== 領主 === 皇室領([[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]→[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]ら)→三浦氏([[三浦泰村]])→皇室領([[後嵯峨天皇|後嵯峨上皇]]ら)
=== 歴史と衰微 === 9世紀の勅旨田荘開発後、背振山門の[[鹿路氏]]や[[廣滝氏]]が経営に関わる。中央から下向してきた藤原氏や平氏の一門もこの地に土着した。藤原氏流[[三瀬氏]]など。[[平安時代]]から[[平氏政権]]期にかけて、大宰府、薩摩阿多と併せ[[日宋貿易]]の拠点となり、[[平忠盛]]一族の知行となる。
[[刀伊の入寇]]後の11 - 12世紀頃、[[鎮西平氏]]の一門[[伊佐平次兼輔]]（[[平兼輔]]）が神埼荘に土着、[[神埼氏]]（神埼兼輔）を称したと伝わる（平兼輔は島津荘を開墾したとされる[[大宰府|大宰大監]][[平季基]]の子と伝わる）。また、[[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]3年（[[1168年]]）に[[荘官]]として[[嵯峨源氏]][[源融|融流]]の[[源満末]]が入る。満末の子孫は[[筑後国]][[三潴郡]][[蒲池]]の[[地頭]]となり[[蒲池氏]]を称する。この頃脊振千坊も栄える（[[霊仙寺跡]]など）。
[[元寇|文永・弘安の役]]で神埼荘は恩賞地配分の対象とされたため細分化された。三島氏族（[[大山祇神社]]）の[[河野氏]]などが入る。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には名前だけを残して荘園としての実態は失われた。
戦国時代に入り肥前に[[龍造寺氏]]が台頭、[[川上峡合戦]]で破れ降り背振山権門は歴史の表舞台からは姿を消した（「[[神代勝利]]」参照）。
== 参考文献 == *『国史大辞典』([[吉川弘文館]]) *『日本史広辞典』([[山川出版社]])
== 関連項目 == * [[日宋貿易]] * [[院政]] * [[平氏政権]] * [[源満末]] * [[日本の荘園の一覧]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:かんさきのしよう}} [[Category:肥前国の荘園]] [[Category:佐賀県域の荘園]] [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:鎌倉時代]] [[Category:平忠盛]]

== 概要 == === 所在 === 肥前国[[神埼郡]]にあったとされる（現在の[[佐賀県]][[神埼市]]付近）
=== 規模 === [[正応]]5年（[[1291年]]）時点、3000町。
=== 起源 === [[承和 (日本)|承和]]3年（[[836年]]）に[[勅旨田]]になった690町が起源である。
[[長和]]4年（[[1015年]]）以前に立荘、皇室領として歴代天皇・上皇に相伝された。

[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には名前だけを残して荘園としての実態は失われた。
戦国時代に入り肥前に[[龍造寺氏]]が台頭、[[川上峡合戦]]で破れ降り背振山権門は歴史の表舞台からは姿を消した（「[[神代勝利]]」参照）。
== 参考文献 == *『国史大辞典』([[吉川弘文館]]) *『日本史広辞典』([[山川出版社]])
== 関連項目 == * [[日宋貿易]] * [[院政]] * [[平氏政権]] * [[源満末]] * [[日本の荘園の一覧]]

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=draft|12=draft}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=draft|12=draft}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=draft|12=draft}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府|12=draft|12=draft}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=draft|12=draft}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=draft|12=draft}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft|12=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=draft|12=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=draft|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=draft|12=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|12=draft|12=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=draft|12=draft}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=draft|12=draft}}

Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "{{DEFAULTSORT:[^]]+}}" to ""
revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} through the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province|ja|神埼郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|ja|神埼市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Size === As of {{ill|Shōō (era)|ja|正応|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|qq=draft|qq=draft}} comprising 690 chō established in {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|ja|承和|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 3 (836). By {{ill|Chōwa (Heian period)|ja|長和|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), transferred to the {{ill|Miura clan|ja|三浦氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft}} alongside {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|ja|平忠盛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Following the {{ill|Tōi invasion|ja|刀伊の入寇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{translated page|ja|神埼荘|version=95844331|comment=Imported full ja history}}

It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|qq=}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|qq=}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|qq=}} branch of the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo Province|qq=}} and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|qq=}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|qq=}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}}, Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamazumi Shrine|qq=}} lineage) receiving portions.
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji clan|qq=}} rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|qq=}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|qq=}}).

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|qq=}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|qq=}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|qq=}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|qq=}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|qq=}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|qq=}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|qq=}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|qq=}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|qq=}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|qq=}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|qq=}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|qq=}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|qq=}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|qq=}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|qq=}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|qq=}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|qq=}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|qq=}}

During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|qq=}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|qq=}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|qq=}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|qq=}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|qq=}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|qq=}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|qq=}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|qq=}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|qq=}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|qq=}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|qq=}}).

revision.text:
{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|qq=}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|qq=}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|qq=}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|qq=}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|qq=}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|qq=}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|qq=}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|qq=}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|qq=}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|qq=}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|qq=}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|qq=}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|qq=}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|qq=}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|qq=}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|qq=}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|qq=}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|qq=}}

Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|qq=}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|qq=}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|qq=}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|qq=}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|qq=}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|qq=}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|qq=}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|qq=}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|qq=}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|qq=}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|qq=}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|qq=}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|qq=}}

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|qq=}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|qq=}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|qq=}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|qq=}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|qq=}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|qq=}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|qq=}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|qq=}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|qq=}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|qq=}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|qq=}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|qq=}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|qq=}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|qq=}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|qq=}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|qq=}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|qq=}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|qq=}}

=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|qq=}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河法皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|qq=}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|qq=}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|qq=}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|qq=}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|qq=}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|qq=}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|qq=}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|qq=}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|qq=}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|qq=}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|qq=}}

{{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
