<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).

In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).

<!--Kanzaki Manor--><!--Kanzaki Manor--> [[Category:Manorial Estates in Saga Region]] [[Category:Taira no Tadamori]] [[Category:Manorial Estates in Hizen Province]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11589659}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|1=List of shōen in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506081}}

During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|1=List of shōen in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506081}}

In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|1=List of shōen in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506081}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|1=List of shōen in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506081}}

=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|1=List of shōen in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506081}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).

It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}

<!--Kanzaki Manor--><!--Kanzaki Manor--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11589659}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Manorial Estates in Hizen Province]] [[Category:Manorial Estates in Saga Region]] [[Category:Taira no Tadamori]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神埼荘]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589659]] Wikidata name: Kanzaki Zhuang [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{混同|神崎荘}} '''Kanzaki-shō''' (神埼荘) was a shōen (manorial estate) in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. It was also known as Kanzaki-shō. The estate was historically part of the imperial domain.
== Overview ==
=== Location === It is believed to have been located in [[:en:Kanzaki District, Saga|Kanzaki District, Hizen Province]], corresponding to present-day [[:en:Kanzaki, Saga|Kanzaki, Saga]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].
=== Size === As of [[:en:Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō (era)]] 5 (1291), it covered approximately 3,000 chō.
=== Origins === The estate originated as a {{ill|Chokushiden|ja|勅旨田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushiden|1=Chokushiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399929}} comprising 690 chō established in [[:en:Jōwa|Jōwa (Heian period)]] 3 (836). By [[:en:Chōwa|Chōwa (Heian period)]] 4 (1015), it had been formalized as a shōen and passed down as an imperial domain.
=== Lords === The estate was part of the imperial domain (held by emperors such as {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317984}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Toba|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後鳥羽天皇|1=Go-Toba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q316658}}), transferred to the [[:en:Miura clan|Miura clan]] (e.g., {{ill|Miura Yasumura|ja|三浦泰村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miura Yasumura|1=Miura Yasumura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11356483}}), and then returned to the imperial domain under figures like {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|ja|後嵯峨上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Saga|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後嵯峨天皇|1=Emperor Go-Saga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q349259}}.
=== History and Decline === After its development as a chokushiden in the 9th century, the estate's management involved local families such as the {{ill|Shikaji clan|ja|鹿路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikaji clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hirotaki clan|ja|廣滝氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirotaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Members of the central aristocracy, such as branches of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]], also settled in the area. These included the {{ill|Mise clan|ja|三瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mise clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who were Fujiwara descendants.
During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. It was controlled by the family of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]].
Following the [[:en:Toi invasion|Tōi invasion]] in the 11th-12th centuries, a branch of the {{ill|Chinzei Taira clan|ja|鎮西平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinzei Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, represented by {{ill|Taira no Kanesuke|ja|平兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja|神埼兼輔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki Kanesuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), settled in Kanzaki-shō. It is said that Kanesuke's lineage became the {{ill|Kanzaki clan|ja|神埼氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanzaki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, claiming descent from {{ill|Taira no Sukemoto|ja|平季基|1=Taira no Suemoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Sukemoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Suemoto|qq=}}, who was involved in the development of Shimazu-shō.
In {{ill|Nin'an|ja|仁安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nin'an|1=Nin'an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18925702}} 3 (1168), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Saga Genji|ja|嵯峨源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga Genji|1=Saga Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477318}} branch of the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], was appointed shōkan (estate administrator). Mitsusue's descendants later became jitō (estate stewards) in the [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]] and founded the {{ill|Kamachi clan|ja|蒲池氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamachi clan|1=Kamachi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621309}}. Around this time, {{ill|Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja|脊振千坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Sefuri's Thousand Temples|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} flourished, as evidenced by sites like the ruins of {{ill|Reisen-ji|ja|霊仙寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisen-ji|1=Ryosen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214098}}.
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]], Kanzaki-shō was divided as reward lands, with families like the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōno clan|qq=}} (descendants of the [[:en:Ōyamazumi Shrine|Ōyamazumi Shrine]] lineage) receiving portions.
By the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the estate had lost its function as a shōen, surviving only in name.
In the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] rose to prominence in Hizen Province. Following their defeat in the {{ill|Battle of Kawakami Gorge|ja|川上峡合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Kawakami Gorge|1=Battle of Kawakamikyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684412}}, the power of Mount Sefuri's temples faded from the historical stage (see {{ill|Jindai Katsutoshi|ja|神代勝利|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jindai Katsutoshi|1=Jindai Katsutoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588686}}).
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=国史大辞典|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日本史広辞典|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Shuppansha|qq=}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} * [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] * {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsusue|ja|源満末|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Mitsusue|1=Minamoto no Mitsusue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564217}} * {{ill|List of shōen in Japan|ja|日本の荘園の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shōen in Japan|1=List of shōen in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506081}}

During the {{ill|Taira clan government|ja|平氏政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan government|1=Taira administration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10827839}}, Kanzaki-shō became a hub for {{ill|Japan-Song Dynasty trade|ja|日宋貿易|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan-Song Dynasty trade|1=Japan–Song trade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10947601}} alongside [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] and Satsuma Ata. === Location === It is believed to have been located in {{ill|Kanzaki District, Saga|en|Kanzaki District, Saga|WD=Q1043882|lt=Kanzaki District, Hizen Province}}, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Kanzaki, Saga|en|Kanzaki, Saga|WD=Q990392|lt=Kanzaki, Saga}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}.
